good: Roll slowly from the armpit down the side of the back, pausing on any tender spots
Breathe out into each tight area and let it release
bad: Rushing through — this is meant to soften the tissue, not bruise it

good: Roller under the upper back — extend back over it one segment at a time
Support the head and keep the ribs from flaring
bad: Cranking from the lower back instead of the mid-back

good: Hands flat, rock gently forward and back to open the wrists
Stay in a pain-free range — this preps the bottom-rack wrist angle
bad: Forcing the stretch or loading it hard

good: Hand behind the head — rotate the elbow up toward the ceiling and follow it with the eyes
Rotate from the mid-back and keep the hips square
bad: Twisting from the lower back or letting the hips rock

good: Anchor the band high, hinge back and sink the chest to lengthen the lat
Relax into it and let the shoulder open
bad: Shrugging the shoulder up toward the ear

good: Reach the hand down the spine and gently guide the elbow back and overhead
Keep the ribs down so the stretch stays in the triceps and lat
bad: Arching the lower back to fake more range

good: Elbows on the bench holding a dowel — sink the chest to open T-spine, lats and triceps at once
Ease a little deeper on each exhale
bad: Bouncing — hold a steady, settled stretch

good: Empty bar in the front rack — actively drive the elbows up to their new ceiling
Move slow and own the top position on every rep
bad: Rushing — the point is to teach the nervous system to hold the range the stretching just opened

good: Hold the front rack on fingertips or three fingers, elbows as high as they'll stay controlled
Film it once a week — elbow height and seconds held are the two numbers to track
bad: Letting the elbows drop or the chest collapse just to hold longer
